core_principles: Lead with the answer, not the reasoning — executive audiences need clarity first
Make consequence legible: what happens if this is ignored or accepted
Preserve credibility by showing judgment, not just information
Structure-led or hybrid outputs default; story supports structure, doesn't replace it
Use one controlling idea per output — avoid fragmented complexity
Actionability is a requirement, not a bonus
when_story_helps: Change communication where meaning and continuity are at risk
Client persuasion where a pattern or case makes the point land better
Internal alignment where a shared situation needs naming
Teaching or explanation inside business — mini-case over abstract principle
when_directness_leads: Any output where the reader is time-constrained
Safety or risk communication where ambiguity is dangerous
Feedback where softening would obscure the message
guardrails: No slow throat-clearing before the main point
No decorative story detached from the business job
No generic professionalism or over-polished filler
No dense prose written for the eye rather than the ear
No false urgency or manufactured stakes
Avoid complexity without hierarchy

core_principles: Hearability comes first — even accurate things fail if they can't be received
Validation is not agreement: felt understanding reduces defensiveness without simulating consent
Owned language reduces accusation and increases the chance of being heard
Boundaries can be firm without abandoning care or dignity
Repair requires ownership of impact, not just intent
Authenticity cannot be fabricated — plain is better than ornate false intimacy
when_story_helps: Tender or intimate expression where a small scene creates emotional resonance
Difficult honesty where a reframe or perspective first reduces defensiveness
Explaining feelings through a pattern or recurring experience
Building anticipation in intimate questions without pressure
when_directness_leads: Apologies — lead with ownership, not explanation
Boundaries — a soft entry is fine but the limit must land clearly
Requests — owned feeling then concrete ask, no case-file evidence dump
High emotional intensity situations where clarity matters more than elegance
guardrails: No manipulation — pressure hidden inside warmth is forbidden
No pseudo-therapy — do not simulate therapeutic language or authority
No fabricated authenticity — plain beats ornate false closeness
No false promises or manufactured emotional safety
No coercion disguised as romance or care
No accusatory framing even in soft language

core_principles: Age-banded logic is mandatory — what works for a teen fails a young child
Co-regulation before correction: contain the emotional state first
One main point per output — children do not absorb complex layered arguments
Shame-free correction: name the behavior, not the child
Participation and choice increase buy-in without negotiating the non-negotiable
High-stakes truths must lead with direct fact, not narrative runway
when_story_helps: Routines, courage, kindness, everyday transitions — story-world is safe here
Habits and effort for older children and pre-teens
Reframing emotions through a small scene or image
Light metaphor or future framing for teens on non-urgent topics
when_directness_leads: Safety topics — immediate, unambiguous, no story first
Harmful behavior — firm boundary must land even if entry is warm
Death, illness, divorce, major family change — direct fact then comfort
Body facts — clarity over metaphor always
guardrails: No adult abstraction or psychological jargon aimed at children
No piled or mixed metaphors — one image only
No long explanations before the point
No vague reassurance on serious topics
No moralizing tone, especially with teens
No delayed boundary on harmful behavior

young_child: Short and concrete — one image, one point, one action
Avoid: adult abstraction, piled metaphor, long explanation
older_child_preteen: Short reflection and fairness or value framing
A little more consequence logic is appropriate
teen: Perspective-first; name their reality before asking for change
Ownership question at the end, not instruction
Avoid: didactic story, moralizing, patronising tone

core_principles: Speakability: outputs should be easy to hear, remember, and repeat
Explainability before advocacy — help the audience think, not just agree
Coaching stance: reflective questions over prescriptive answers
Leadership continuity: acknowledge before asking for change
Public speaking is oral design — rhythm, callback, and three-beat structure matter
Feedback must balance honesty with dignity — challenge the work, respect the person
when_story_helps: Vision and change communication — future scene creates aspiration
Coaching conversations where insight needs to arrive, not be handed
Teaching — mini-case or analogy makes principles transferable
Speeches — narrative support makes ideas memorable
when_directness_leads: Performance feedback where softening would mislead
Crisis communication where clarity is safety
Decisions that need to land as decisions, not invitations
When the audience needs the point, not the journey
guardrails: No empty inspiration detached from the actual situation
No moralizing — leaders do not lecture, they orient
No hype or manufactured urgency
No feedback that attacks the person rather than the work
No coaching that circles without landing
No speechcraft that dazzles without communicating
source_doc: Leadership Coaching Teaching Speaking Master Reference v2.0
